en.extract:
The phainopepla or northern phainopepla (Phainopepla nitens) is the most northerly representative of the mainly tropical Central American family Ptiliogonatidae, the silky flycatchers. Its name is from the Greek phain pepla meaning "shining robe" in reference to the male's plumage.


Description
The phainopepla is a striking bird, 16–20 cm (6.3–7.9 in) long with a noticeable crest and a long tail; it is slender, and has an upright posture when it perches. Its bill is short and slender. The male is glossy black, and has a white wing patch that is visible when it flies; the female is plain gray and has a lighter gray wing patch. Both sexes have red eyes, but these are more noticeable in the female than the male.


Range and habitat
The phainopepla ranges as far north as central California with the San Joaquin Valley and southern Utah, and south to central Mexico, the interior Mexican Plateau region; the southern edge of the plateau, the transverse mountains is its non-breeding home. It is found in hot areas, including desert oases, and is readily seen in the deserts of Arizona, southern Nevada, and southern California; also the Baja Peninsula, both Baja California-(north), and Baja California Sur where they are the only breeding resident birds. Extreme individuals have travelled as far as Canada, with one bird in 2009 reaching as far north as Brampton, Ontario, Canada.


Diet
Their diet consists of berries, any small insects, fruits, vegetables. Phainopeplas have a specialized mechanism in their gizzard that shucks berry skins off the fruit and packs the skins separately from the rest of the fruit into the intestines for more efficient digestion. So far this is the only known bird able to do this. They appear to relish the fruit of Phoradendron californicum, the desert mistletoe.


Symbiosis with desert mistletoe
Phainopepla are closely associated with desert mistletoe, and are the most effective dispersers of its seeds. As a mistletoe specialist, phainopepla have a specialized digestive system, and process berries very quickly without the gizzard crushing the seeds; berries are defecated 12–45 minutes after being eaten. As a result, each bird can process hundreds of berries in a day. Phainopepla derive fewer calories from each berry than non-specialist birds, but this ability to eat and quickly process large number of berries allows them to meet their daily caloric requirements, while non-specialist birds may not be able to do so on just mistletoe berries. Phainopepla deposit their feces on tree branches. In doing so, the mistletoe seeds within have a host tree to infect after they sprout.


Reproduction
It nests in the spring. They make loosely constructed nests of twigs, mosses, plant fibers, placed on branches of trees, usually below 20 feet from the ground, in thickets or open woods near water. The eggs are gray or pink and speckled, and the incubation, done by both the male and female, takes fifteen days. The young will be reared by the parents for up to nineteen more days.


Song
Phainopeplas have been found to imitate the calls of twelve other species, such as the red-tailed hawk (Buteo jamaicensis) and the northern flicker (Colaptes auratus).


References


Further reading


Books
Chu, M.; Walsberg, G. (1999). "Phainopepla (Phainopepla nitens)". In Poole, A.; Gill, F. (eds.). The Birds of North America. Philadelphia, PA: The Birds of North America, Inc.


External links

BirdLife species factsheet for Phainopepla nitens
"Phainopepla nitens". Avibase. 
"Phainopepla media". Internet Bird Collection.
Phainopepla photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Interactive range map of Phainopepla nitens at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of Phainopepla on Xeno-canto.

The '''phainopepla''' or '''northern phainopepla'''<ref name=Weathers1983/> ('''''Phainopepla nitens''''') is the most northerly representative of the mainly tropical [[Central America]]n family [[Ptiliogonatidae]], the [[silky flycatcher]]s. Its name is from the Greek {{Transliteration|grc|phain pepla}} meaning "shining robe" in reference to the male's plumage.

==Description==
The phainopepla is a striking [[bird]], {{convert|16|–|20|cm|in|abbr=on}} long with a noticeable [[Crest (feathers)|crest]] and a long tail; it is slender, and has an upright posture when it perches. Its [[Beak|bill]] is short and slender. The male is glossy black, and has a white wing patch that is visible when it flies; the female is plain gray and has a lighter gray wing patch. Both sexes have red eyes, but these are more noticeable in the female than the male.

==Range and habitat==
The phainopepla ranges as far north as central [[California]] with the [[San Joaquin Valley]] and southern [[Utah]], and south to central [[Mexico]], the interior [[Mexican Plateau]] region; the southern edge of the plateau, the [[Trans-Mexican Volcanic Belt|transverse mountains]] is its non-breeding home. It is found in hot areas, including desert oases, and is readily seen in the deserts of [[Arizona]], southern [[Nevada]], and southern [[California]]; also the [[Baja Peninsula]], both [[Baja California]]-(north), and [[Baja California Sur]] where they are the only breeding [[resident bird]]s.{{Citation needed|date=April 2019}} Extreme individuals have travelled as far as Canada, with one bird in 2009 reaching as far north as [[Brampton]], Ontario, Canada.<ref name="brampton" />

==Diet==
Their diet consists of berries, any small insects, fruits, vegetables. Phainopeplas have a specialized mechanism in their [[gizzard]] that shucks berry skins off the fruit and packs the skins separately from the rest of the fruit into the intestines for more efficient digestion.{{citation needed|date=May 2020}} So far this is the only known bird able to do this. They appear to relish the fruit of ''[[Phoradendron californicum]]'', the desert mistletoe.

=== Symbiosis with desert mistletoe ===
Phainopepla are closely associated with desert mistletoe, and are the most effective dispersers of its seeds.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Aukema |first1=Juliann E. |last2=Martínez del Rio |first2=Carlos |date=2002 |title=Where Does a Fruit-Eating Bird Deposit Mistletoe Seeds? Seed Deposition Patterns and an Experiment |url=https://esajournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1890/0012-9658%282002%29083%5B3489%3AWDAFEB%5D2.0.CO%3B2 |journal=Ecology |language=en |volume=83 |issue=12 |pages=3489–3496 |doi=10.1890/0012-9658(2002)083[3489:WDAFEB]2.0.CO;2 |issn=1939-9170|url-access=subscription }}</ref> As a mistletoe specialist, phainopepla have a specialized digestive system, and process berries very quickly without the gizzard crushing the seeds; berries are defecated 12–45 minutes after being eaten.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Walsberg |first=Glenn E. |date=1975-04-01 |title=Digestive Adaptations of Phainopepla Nitens Associated with the Eating of Mistletoe Berries |url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/77/2/169/5205486 |journal=The Condor |volume=77 |issue=2 |pages=169–174 |doi=10.2307/1365787 |jstor=1365787 |issn=1938-5129|url-access=subscription }}</ref> As a result, each bird can process hundreds of berries in a day.<ref name=":0" /> Phainopepla derive fewer calories from each berry than non-specialist birds, but this ability to eat and quickly process large number of berries allows them to meet their daily caloric requirements, while non-specialist birds may not be able to do so on just mistletoe berries.<ref name=":1" /> Phainopepla deposit their feces on tree branches. In doing so, the mistletoe seeds within have a host tree to infect after they sprout.<ref name=":0" />

==Reproduction==
It nests in the spring. They make loosely constructed nests of twigs, mosses, plant fibers, placed on branches of trees, usually below 20 feet from the ground, in thickets or open woods near water.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Bird Book |url=https://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/30000/pg30000-images.html#Page_375}}</ref> The eggs are gray or pink and speckled, and the incubation, done by both the male and female, takes fifteen days. The young will be reared by the parents for up to nineteen more days.<ref name="andiv" />[[File:Phainopepla (Phainopepla nitens) - Flickr - Lip Kee.jpg|thumb|Male in flight showing white wing patches]]

==Song==
Phainopeplas have been found to imitate the calls of twelve other species, such as the [[red-tailed hawk]] (''Buteo jamaicensis'') and the [[northern flicker]] (''Colaptes auratus'').<ref name="Chu2001" />
